rules.description: Every system must be understood through three lenses: Purpose, Dependency, Exposure (Tri-Nature).
The Tri-Nature pattern is fractal — it repeats at system, sub-system, service, validation, and exposure level.
Design every part with awareness that it will become someone else's dependency or exposure.
The engineering sequence is fixed: Purposing → Modeling → Simulation. Never skip or reorder.
Never start implementation when purpose is unclear.
Never start modeling when the problem observation has not been completed.
Start every design session with observation: identify the real blocker, constraint, or unmet need.
Articulate the problem clearly before proposing a solution.
Solutioning must honor: readability, configurability, longevity, optimization, and maintainability.
Reaching the goal the wrong way is a violation — never cut corners.
If purpose is unclear, stop and clarify before proceeding to modeling or implementation.
Model only what the purpose requires — no speculative attributes.
Do not attract irrelevant attributes into the model.
Prefer the most generic valid name that still fits the problem scope.
Primary models must be self-sufficient — they must not physically depend on another model to exist.
Secondary models must reference or nest within a primary model.
Relational models connect exactly two primary models; they must not contain unrelated details.
Hybrid models are allowed only when the business flow truly requires mixing relational + additional details.
Integration models = brokers. Processing models = services. Exposure models = exposers. Configuration models = startup/middleware.
Simulation must stay inside the purpose and model boundaries.
Functions, methods, and routines are the simulation mechanisms — they must not cross model ownership.
Simplicity is mandatory, not optional.
Do not use more than one level of inheritance (excessive inheritance is prohibited).
No Utils, no Commons, no Helpers — these create horizontal entanglement.
No shared models across independent flows.
No local base components that create hidden coupling (vertical entanglement).
Prefer duplication over cross-flow entanglement when duplication preserves autonomy.
Every component must be self-sufficient — it owns its validations, tooling, and utilities.
Components may depend on injected dependencies, not hidden helpers.
Duplication is permitted when it preserves ownership and autonomy.
No hidden routines.
No magical extensions that require chasing references to understand.
No runtime tricks that make the system hard to understand.
Validation, exception handling, tracing, security, and localization must be in plain sight.
Every system must be easy to understand, modify, and fully rewrite.
No hidden dependencies.
No unknown prerequisites.
No injected routines that obscure behavior.
Code must be understandable by entry-level engineers.
If new engineers cannot follow the system, the system is too complex.
The Standard must be embraced fully or rejected fully — partial adoption is not standardization.
Outdated partial adherence does not constitute a Standardized system.
When in doubt, choose readability over performance.
Unreadable 'optimal' software is not truly optimum.
The system must be runnable locally without mandatory cloud dependency.
Develop tooling that bridges cloud resources to local stand-ins.
Do not enforce Standard compliance via style cops or analyzers as the primary mechanism.
Teach The Standard person-to-person, not through coercion tools.
Work every day as if it might be your last day on the project.
End each engineering day at a good stopping point so another engineer can continue seamlessly.
